data.title: Eaton: Growth Is Largely Priced In
data.description:
Shares of Eaton (ETN) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, losing about 9% of their value. While its core business is doing well, there is already substantial optimism priced into shares. When I last covered shares in August, I rated Eaton a hold. While I felt valuation was “getting stretched” and pricing in a “fairly perfect outcome,” I felt the momentum of the AI boom was too strong to sell shares. With the stock down 4% even as the market has gained 6%, my valuation concerns were reasonable and arguably should have driven a sell recommendation. After this modest pullback, now is a good time to see if underperformance will continue or if shares can rebound as we head into 2026.

Looking first at recent results, in the company’s third quarter reported on November 4th, Eaton earned $2.59, which was $0.15 below expectations. While revenue grew by 10% to $7 billion, this was also $90 million below consensus. Back in August, I wrote ETN would need to deliver beats and raises to continue the rally in shares, and this past quarter fell short of that, even as ETN maintained full-year guidance, suggesting the misses were entirely timing-based, and organic sales growth was 7%.

To be clear, recent results were not weak; rather, ETN has been a victim of very high expectations. On the positive side, its book-to-bill ratio was 1.1x for the 12 months ending in Q3 as the company built more backlog. Keeping this ratio above 1x is essential to maintain revenue growth into the future and speaks to the strong demand ETN has across most end markets. Electrical Americas orders surged to $4 billion in Q3. Its electrical backlog is now 4x larger than its historic level.

At a segment level, Electrical Americas revenue was up 15% to $3.4 billion, and margins expanded 20 bps to 30.3%. Its backlog is now $12 billion. Its Electric Global unit grew revenue 10% to $1.7 billion, with margins also expanding 40 bps to 19.1%. Data center spending is disproportionately focused in the US, which is why its US segment has faster growth.

Aerospace continues to gain momentum, reaching a record with sales up 14% to $1.08 billion. Margins also were up an excellent 150 bps to 25.9%. Its backlog is up an equally strong 15%, and orders rose 11% in Q3. Despite challenges across the broader aerospace supply chain, ETN has managed this unit extremely well, gaining market share, and I see the outlook here as also being bright.

Vehicle sales fell by 8% to $639 million, and margins compressed 160 bps to 17.8%. Given tariffs and muted consumer demand, volumes have been challenged here. While we are likely nearing a bottom in my view, any recovery is likely to be slow, with stability a more reasonable target.

Looking forward over the near term, while speaking at a conference on December 2nd, Eaton affirmed it is “on track” to deliver guidance, which should alleviate concerns that Q3 pressures would persist. The company does see softness in the auto and residential construction markets. I expect Q4 results to come in at least at the midpoint of guidance given December commentary. Back in August, I was targeting $3.8-$4 billion of free cash flow this year. I still view this as the central case.

Shares offer a 1.2% dividend yield. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is also down 2.1% from last year. The company maintains an excellent balance sheet with less than $10 billion of debt. As such, Eaton is well positioned to continue returning capital to investors and doing bolt-on M&A, like its recent Boyd purchase, with its free cash flow.

Separately, in November, Eaton announced its CFO would be leaving in April 2026 to enable an orderly transition. The company will be searching for a successor both internally and externally, and I like to see a replacement announced by February to provide some overlap and continuity. I do not expect a change in financial policy as a result, and this orderly transition does not alter my outlook for shares.

More broadly, at the December conference, management discussed 2026, saying it expects its end market to grow about 7% and anticipates outperforming this. With incremental margins of ~30%, this implies 10-12% operating income growth next year. With some share repurchases, this leaves me expecting $13.60-$14 of EPS next year and $4.4-$4.5 billion of free cash flow. The company is enjoying secular tailwinds that support this growth.

Namely, as you can see below, technology capex spending has surged in recent years, with large tech firms set to basically triple their 2024 spending by 2027. Under their current plans, we should see spending rise over 30% next year vs. their year. Increased data center spending is a significant tailwind for Eaton’s business and has become a driving source of its growth. I expect orders and revenue to grow at least for several years.

I do remain cautious on whether all anticipated spending occurs. Aside from long-term views on AI, there are finite amounts of construction crews, supply chains for materials, etc., which may simply make it difficult to build out data centers as quickly as each company hopes to. While all of this spending may occur, there is a risk it takes longer to build out facilities. Additionally, as companies like Oracle (ORCL) and OpenAI become increasingly reliant on external financing to meet buildout plans, that adds another layer of risk to long-term growth. Overall, even if we undershoot plans beyond 2026, Eaton is likely to benefit from rising data center demand for years. But given capacity constraints, the pace of growth is unlikely to accelerate, and if anything, it should steadily slow.

Elsewhere, Eaton’s aerospace unit should continue to benefit from the turnaround at Boeing (BA), which appears to be gaining traction. As Boeing’s production continues to improve, that should filter through to more orders and revenue for Eaton, providing an additional leg of growth for the company.

On the other hand, I am cautious about Eaton’s outlook for slight growth in the residential market. As you can see below, the number of homes under construction has been steadily declining. With the levels of starts muted, we are likely to see declines continue naturally at least into mid-2026 before bottoming. I believe we are approaching a bottom in residential construction but are not quite there yet, so ETN’s prediction may prove a little early. Still, this is a small piece of the business, and assuming the ~3% decline I expect, this would be a sub-0.5% impact on Eaton’s overall growth prospects.

Still, the outlook is bright, but much is priced into shares. Eaton should generate ~$11.75-$12 free cash flow in 2025, giving shares a ~3.5% free cash flow yield. The stock is also trading at ~24x 2026 earnings. With upper single-digit growth, ETN is well positioned to grow into this multiple over time and remains a lower beta way to play the tech buildout. That said, I believe it is too early to start buying shares, especially after an earnings miss in its most recent quarter. I continue to view $325 as a buy point, and if shares were to pull back there, I would be a buyer. For now, I remain a “hold” and recommend patience before adding.

data.title: The S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index: A Comprehensive Approach To The Leveraged Loan Market
data.description:
By Eric Pettinelli

The S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index is a new entrant in the leveraged loan landscape, developed to fill a critical gap in existing liquid leveraged loan index offerings that typically focus on the largest loans by market capitalization. This innovative index encompasses all USD-denominated fully funded term loans with a minimum facility size of USD 500 million and credit ratings below investment grade, providing a broader view of the market.

As the U.S. leveraged loan market surged to an estimated USD 1.5 trillion in July 2025, the diversity of borrowers expanded significantly, incorporating a wide range of loan types, credit qualities, spreads, covenants and other characteristics. This variety is often not fully captured when analyzing only the top 100 largest loans in the market. The back-tested performance of the index was positive over the last five years, as showcased in Exhibit 1.

This index not only provides a more holistic and representative view of the liquid leveraged loan market by including loans of USD 500 million and above, but also enhances sector diversification and risk/performance profile with a total of 930 constituents ending October 2025.

Examining the credit quality characteristics of loans beyond the top 100 by notional index value offers deeper insights into the broader market. These insights include tracking defaults of underlying loans, which serve as crucial early warning signs in the leveraged loan ecosystem—information that may be missed when focusing solely on the largest loans.

Additionally, potential risks such as credit deterioration become more evident with a larger dataset, allowing investors and market observers to make more informed decisions. The index also incorporates higher spreads into the overall mix, driven by the inclusion of smaller loans and lower average credit quality. Exhibit 2 illustrates the difference in credit quality between the top 100 loans in the S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index and the rest of the constituents as of October 2025.

The effects of broader loan inclusion also extend to sector profile, as the addition of lower notional value loans provides access to a wider array of business types within the index, as shown in Exhibit 3.1 For an index that focuses on including lower credit quality borrowers, diversifying across industries can have a positive effect, not only for encompassing additional sources of performance but also for moderating risk.

In summary, the S&P USD Select Leveraged Loan Index offers a benchmark for measuring larger loans within the leveraged loan universe without sacrificing the risk/performance balance and diversification qualities that result from the additional inclusion of smaller issuances. As the leveraged loan market continues to evolve and expand, indices that adopt a comprehensive view of the leveraged loan market will serve as essential tools for more informed investment decisions.

1 The S&P USD Select Leveraged Loans Index follows the iBoxx Industry Classification Standard (iBICs). For further details, please see iBICs | S&P Dow Jones Indices
